title: Activity, Half-Life, and Radioactive Decay
narrationScript: A gamma source is a population of unstable atoms, each waiting to decay. You cannot say when any single nucleus will go, but with billions upon billions of them, the population follows a schedule so dependable that we plan exposures, surveys, and shipping papers around it. This lesson gives you the units and the math. First, the unit of activity. Activity means decay rate, how many nuclei are disintegrating each second. The international unit is the becquerel, abbreviated B q, and it is the simplest unit in radiography. One becquerel is one disintegration per second. Because a useful source disintegrates fantastically fast, field sources are quoted in gigabecquerels, billions of disintegrations per second, or terabecquerels, trillions. The older unit you will still see on most source certificates in North America is the curie. One curie is thirty seven billion disintegrations per second, which is exactly thirty seven gigabecquerels. So a one hundred curie source is three point seven terabecquerels, and either number describes the same physical fact, the same number of nuclei popping each second. Keep one distinction sharp. Activity is not dose rate. Activity tells you how fast the source is decaying. The radiation field around it also depends on what each decay emits, which is why one curie of cobalt sixty throws a much hotter field than one curie of iridium one ninety two. Second, half-life. Decay is statistical, and every radioisotope has a fixed characteristic time in which half of any starting population decays. That time is the half-life. For iridium one ninety two it is about seventy four days. For cobalt sixty, about five and a quarter years. For selenium seventy five, about one hundred twenty days. The rule never changes. After one half-life, half remains. After two, a quarter. After three, an eighth. The cleanest way to work decay problems in your head is the halving table. Write the starting activity, then halve it once for every half-life that has passed. Say your iridium source was assayed at one hundred curies. Seventy four days later it is fifty. One hundred forty eight days after assay it is twenty five. Two hundred twenty two days, twelve and a half. Notice the source never reaches zero on paper. It just keeps halving, which is why even a source years past useful strength still demands respect and proper disposal. When the elapsed time is not a whole number of half-lives, the formula behind the table is activity equals starting activity times one half raised to the power of elapsed time divided by half-life. For field work, bracket the answer between the two nearest whole half-lives, or read it from a decay chart. Third, plotting. Put decay on ordinary graph paper, activity against time, and you get a falling curve that drops steeply at first and flattens as it goes, the classic exponential. Useful, but awkward to read precisely. Replot the same data on semilogarithmic paper, where the activity axis is logarithmic, and the curve becomes a dead straight line. That is the trick behind the decay charts hanging in every source storage area. A straight line is easy to draw from two points and easy to read at any date in between, so the standard practice is to plot the certified activity at the assay date and let the line predict today's curies. Crews check today's activity against that chart before computing exposure time, because exposure time stretches in exact proportion as activity falls. A shot that took two minutes at one hundred curies takes four minutes at fifty and eight minutes at twenty five. Fourth, specific activity. Two sources can hold the same total activity in very different amounts of metal. Specific activity is activity per unit mass, becquerels per gram or curies per gram, and it measures how richly the material was activated. High specific activity means the same curies fit in a smaller pellet, and a smaller pellet behaves more like the ideal point source, casting sharper shadows on the film. Low specific activity means a physically larger source and more geometric unsharpness. That is why source makers chase long activation times in high neutron flux, and why a premium iridium source is a tiny stack of pellets a couple of millimeters across. Learn the becquerel and the curie, own the halving table, trust the straight line on semilog paper, and remember that specific activity is the bridge between the physics of this lesson and the sharpness of your radiographs.
imagePrompt: A radiographer at a steel work table updates a wall-mounted source decay chart with a pencil and ruler beside a closed gamma projector, his calculator and source certificate laid out under a work lamp.

sections.heading: The Becquerel: Counting Decays per Second
sections.body:
**Activity** is the rate at which a radioactive source decays - how many nuclei disintegrate each second. The SI unit is the **becquerel (Bq)**: **1 Bq = 1 disintegration per second (dps)**. It is an honest but tiny unit; working sources are quoted in **gigabecquerels (GBq, 10⁹ dps)** or **terabecquerels (TBq, 10¹² dps)**.

The traditional unit still printed on most North American source certificates is the **curie (Ci)**: **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps = 37 GBq**. The conversions worth memorizing:

- 1 Ci = 37 GBq
- 100 Ci = 3.7 TBq
- 1 TBq ≈ 27 Ci

Activity is **not** dose rate. Activity counts decays; the radiation field around the source also depends on what each decay emits. One curie of Co-60 (two high-energy gammas per decay) produces roughly 1.3 R/h at one meter, while one curie of Ir-192 produces roughly 0.5 R/h at one meter - same activity, very different field. Emissivity gets its own treatment in the next module; here, nail down that the becquerel and the curie measure decay rate only.

sections.heading: Half-Life: The Fixed Clock
sections.body:
Decay is statistical. No one can say when a particular nucleus will disintegrate, but for the billions in a source the population behavior is exact: in one **half-life (T½)**, half of any starting quantity decays. The half-life is a fixed signature of each nuclide - unchangeable by heat, pressure, or chemistry:

- **Ir-192: ≈ 74 days**
- **Co-60: ≈ 5.27 years**
- **Se-75: ≈ 120 days**
- **Cs-137: ≈ 30 years**

The working math is the **halving table**: halve the assay activity once per elapsed half-life. A 100 Ci Ir-192 source is 50 Ci after 74 days, 25 Ci after 148 days, 12.5 Ci after 222 days, 6.25 Ci after 296 days. For times between whole half-lives, the governing equation is:

**A = A₀ × (½)^(t/T½)**

where A₀ is the activity at assay and t is elapsed time. On paper the activity never reaches zero - it only keeps halving - so an 'old, dead' source is never actually dead. It is merely weaker, and it still requires licensed control and proper disposal.

sections.caption: The same Ir-192 decay plotted two ways: an exponential curve on linear axes, and a straight line on a semilog activity scale - the form used for field decay charts.

sections.heading: Plotting Decay: Why Semilog Paper Wins
sections.body:
Plot activity against time on ordinary **linear** graph paper and you get the classic exponential: a curve that falls steeply at first and flattens forever after. It shows the behavior, but reading a precise value off a curve is clumsy.

Replot the same data on **semilogarithmic** paper - time on the linear axis, activity on a logarithmic axis - and exponential decay becomes a **straight line**. That is the entire trick behind the decay charts posted wherever sources are stored. Because a straight line is defined by two points, the chart is drawn from the certificate: plot the assayed activity on the assay date, plot half that value one half-life later, connect, and extend. Today's activity is read directly off the line.

The reading matters because **exposure time scales inversely with activity**. A weld shot needing 2 minutes at 100 Ci needs 4 minutes at 50 Ci and 8 minutes at 25 Ci. Crews who skip the decay chart and reuse last month's exposure time produce thin, underexposed film - and reshoots cost more than the thirty seconds the chart takes.

sections.title: The halving table is your field calculator
sections.body: Assay: **100 Ci** Ir-192 (T½ ≈ 74 days). Then: 74 d → **50 Ci**, 148 d → **25 Ci**, 222 d → **12.5 Ci**, 296 d → **6.25 Ci**. Halve once per half-life, and bracket between halvings for in-between dates. Three half-lives always leaves **1/8** of the start; that fraction is the same for every nuclide - only the length of the half-life differs.

sections.heading: Specific Activity: Curies per Gram
sections.body:
Two sources can hold identical total activity in very different amounts of metal. **Specific activity** is activity per unit mass - **becquerels per gram (Bq/g)** in SI, curies per gram in traditional units - and it states how richly the material was activated in the reactor.

Why a radiographer cares:

- **Source size.** High specific activity packs the required curies into a smaller pellet stack. A modern high-specific-activity Ir-192 source of around 100 Ci is only a few millimeters across.
- **Image sharpness.** A smaller source behaves more like an ideal point source. Geometric unsharpness grows in direct proportion to source physical size, so for the same curies, the higher-specific-activity source makes the sharper radiograph.
- **What sets it.** Neutron flux density and irradiation time during production (lesson rt1-2-1), plus decay afterward - as a source decays, its specific activity falls with its activity while the metal mass stays put.

When a procedure or purchase spec lists both the activity **and** the maximum physical source size, it is really controlling specific activity - buying sharpness, not just strength.

sections.caption: Activity units and specific activity: the curie-becquerel ladder, and why the same 100 Ci in a smaller pellet means a sharper image.

sections.heading: Working Decay Problems Like a Technician
sections.body:
A reliable routine for any decay question:

1. **Find the elapsed time** between the assay date on the certificate and today.
2. **Divide by the half-life** to get the number of half-lives, n = t / T½.
3. **Halve the assay activity n times** (or apply A = A₀ × (½)ⁿ). For fractional n, bracket between the neighboring whole halvings or read the semilog chart.
4. **Adjust the technique**: new exposure time = old time × (old activity / new activity).

Worked example: a 120 Ci Ir-192 source assayed March 1 is used on July 13 - about 134 days, so n = 134 / 74 ≈ 1.8 half-lives. Bracket: after 1 half-life, 60 Ci; after 2, 30 Ci. The chart reads about **34 Ci**. A technique that took 1.5 minutes at assay strength now needs 120/34 ≈ 3.5 times longer - call it 5.3 minutes. That is the entire practical life of a decay curve: certificate in, today's curies out, exposure time corrected before the first shot of the shift.

figures.title: Decay curve: linear versus semilog plot

interactive.title: The Decayed Source
interactive.intro: Monday morning. Your crew's Ir-192 source (half-life about 74 days) was assayed at 120 Ci exactly 74 days ago. The job folder still shows exposure times calculated on the assay date. Walk through the decisions before the first shot.

steps.prompt: Before anything else, what is the source's activity today, 74 days after its 120 Ci assay?
options: 120 Ci - activity only changes when the source is used
60 Ci - one half-life has elapsed
30 Ci - two half-lives have elapsed
90 Ci - it loses a quarter per half-life
steps.feedback: 74 days is exactly one half-life for Ir-192, so half the assayed activity remains: 120 Ci becomes 60 Ci. Decay runs on the calendar, not on usage - the source decays the same whether it ever leaves the projector or not.

steps.prompt: The folder's technique sheet calls for a 2-minute exposure based on 120 Ci. With 60 Ci today, what should the exposure be?
options: 1 minute - less activity needs less time
Still 2 minutes - the chart was qualified
4 minutes - time scales inversely with activity
8 minutes - time scales with the square of the decay
steps.feedback: Exposure (in curie-minutes) must stay the same, so time scales inversely with activity: half the curies, twice the minutes. 2 min × (120/60) = 4 minutes. Using the stale 2-minute figure would produce thin, underexposed film.

steps.prompt: The client's paperwork asks for today's activity in SI units. 60 Ci is:
options: 2.22 TBq, since 1 Ci = 37 GBq
60 GBq, since curies and gigabecquerels are equal
6.0 TBq, since 1 Ci = 100 GBq
222 MBq, since 1 Ci = 3.7 MBq
steps.feedback: 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps = 37 GBq. So 60 Ci × 37 GBq/Ci = 2,220 GBq = 2.22 TBq. The becquerel is one disintegration per second, so field sources land in the GBq-TBq range.

steps.prompt: Your helper suggests 'recharging' the source overnight in its shielded container so exposures get shorter again. Your call?
options: Good idea - shielding slows the decay rate
Decay cannot be paused or reversed; plan around the decay chart and schedule a source exchange when times become impractical
Heating the projector would restore some activity
Using the source less often will let activity recover
steps.feedback: Nothing outside the nucleus - shielding, temperature, storage, usage - changes the decay schedule. Activity only falls. When exposure times grow uneconomical (typically after one to two half-lives), the licensee exchanges the decayed source for a fresh one.

checkQuestions.question: One becquerel equals:
options: One disintegration per second
37 billion disintegrations per second
One disintegration per minute
One roentgen per hour at one meter
checkQuestions.explanation: The becquerel is the SI unit of activity: 1 Bq = 1 dps. The curie is the large traditional unit, 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps = 37 GBq.

checkQuestions.question: An Ir-192 source is assayed at 80 Ci. About 222 days later (three half-lives of 74 days), its activity is:
options: 40 Ci
20 Ci
10 Ci
checkQuestions.explanation: Halve once per half-life: 80 → 40 → 20 → 10 Ci. Three half-lives always leaves one-eighth of the starting activity.

checkQuestions.question: Plotted on semilogarithmic paper (logarithmic activity axis), radioactive decay appears as:
options: A straight line
A steepening curve
A bell curve
A horizontal line
checkQuestions.explanation: Exponential decay becomes a straight line when activity is plotted on a log scale, which is why field decay charts use semilog form - easy to draw from the assay point and easy to read on any date.

checkQuestions.question: Specific activity is defined as:
options: The dose rate at one meter per curie
Activity per unit mass, such as becquerels per gram
The activity remaining after one half-life
The number of gammas emitted per disintegration
checkQuestions.explanation: Specific activity is activity divided by mass (Bq/g or Ci/g). High specific activity packs the needed curies into a smaller pellet, which reduces geometric unsharpness.

checkQuestions.question: Two Ir-192 sources each contain 100 Ci, but source A's pellet stack is half the physical size of source B's. Which statement is correct?
options: Source A has lower specific activity and makes sharper images
Source B has higher specific activity because it is larger
Source A has higher specific activity and will produce less geometric unsharpness
Both have the same specific activity because the activities are equal
checkQuestions.explanation: Same activity in less mass means higher Ci/g. The physically smaller source acts more like a point source, so its shadows - the images of discontinuities - are sharper.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 3.3 (becquerel - the unit of activity)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 3.4 (half-life), 3.5 (plotting of radioactive decay)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Basic Radiographic Physics 3.6 (specific activity - becquerels/gram)
Conversion: 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ dps = 37 GBq; half-lives Ir-192 ≈ 74 d, Co-60 ≈ 5.27 yr, Se-75 ≈ 120 d (reference values)
